request.system:
You are a regulatory analyst. Given a prediction-market contract and a single regulatory news record, decide if this record is relevant to whether the contract resolves YES or NO. Score relevance 0-10 (0 = off-topic, 10 = directly determinative). Return JSON matching the schema. one_line_why should be ≤160 chars and explain how this record moves resolution probability.

Direction: judge whether this event makes the contract MORE likely to resolve YES (= bullish), LESS likely to resolve YES (= bearish), or has no directional effect (= neutral). IMPORTANT: direction is always relative to YES resolution. If your one_line_why says the event 'increases odds' or 'raises probability', direction MUST be bullish. If it says 'decreases odds' or 'lowers probability', direction MUST be bearish. Do not contradict yourself.

Magnitude: high = materially changes probability, medium = notable but not decisive, low = incremental signal.

Timeline shift: does this event suggest resolution will come sooner or later than expected, or no change (none)?
request.user:
Contract title: SCOTUS rules Trump can fire FTC commissioners?
Resolution criteria: This market will resolve to 'Yes' if the U.S. Supreme Court rules in Trump v.
Slaughter (or a consolidated companion case) that the President may remove Federal
Trade Commission commissioners without cause, by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET.
Resolution requires a majority opinion from the Supreme Court. Otherwise, this
market will resolve to 'No'.

Background: President Trump fired FTC Commissioners Rebecca Kelly Slaughter and
Alvaro Bedoya in early 2025. The case challenges the 1935 Humphrey's Executor
precedent, which prohibits presidents from removing commissioners of independent
agencies without cause. A ruling for Trump would fundamentally restructure the
FTC's independence from executive control and reshape antitrust enforcement authority.

The Supreme Court's term ends in June 2026; a decision is expected before then.
Market currently prices a 97% probability of YES.

Conditions:
  A: SCOTUS majority in Trump v. Slaughter — By Dec 31, 2026, SCOTUS issues a majority opinion in Trump v. Slaughter holding the President may remove FTC commissioners without cause.
  B: SCOTUS majority in companion case — By Dec 31, 2026, SCOTUS issues a majority opinion in a consolidated companion case holding the President may remove FTC commissioners without cause.
Settlement entities: ['Supreme Court of the United States', 'Federal Trade Commission', 'Department of Justice', 'Office of the Solicitor General']

Record title: Attorney General Bonta Co-Leads Bipartisan Coalition in Demanding GoFundMe Prove Removal of All Plagiarized Web Pages Using Charities’ Information
Record date: 2026-03-03
Record regulator: California Attorney General
Record topic: Office of the Attorney General, California Department of Justice
Record entities: ['Al Schmidt', 'Andrea Joy Campbell', 'Anthony G. Brown', 'California Attorney General', 'Charity R. Clark', 'Dan Rayfield', 'David W. Sunday, Jr.', 'Delaware Attorney General', 'Gentner Drummond', 'Illinois Attorney General', 'Jeff Jackson', 'Jennifer Davenport', 'John M. Formella', 'Joshua L. Kaul', 'Kathleen Jennings', 'Keith Ellison', 'Kentucky Attorney General', 'Letitia James', 'Mark Hammond', 'Maryland Attorney General', 'Massachusetts Attorney General', 'Michigan Attorney General', 'Minnesota Attorney General', 'New Hampshire Attorney General', 'New Jersey Attorney General', 'New Mexico Attorney General', 'New York Attorney General', 'Nick Brown', 'North Carolina Attorney General', 'Oklahoma Attorney General', 'Pennsylvania Attorney General', 'Pennsylvania Secretary of the Commonwealth', 'Rob Bonta', 'Russell Coleman', 'South Carolina Secretary of State', 'Tennessee Secretary of State', 'Tre Hargett', 'Vermont Attorney General', 'Vermont Office of the Attorney General', 'Washington Attorney General', 'Wisconsin Attorney General']

response.one_line_why: State AGs' complaint to GoFundMe about plagiarized charity pages is unrelated to the Supreme Court removal case, so it doesn't change YES odds.
